current_fact.fact_text: On 20 March 1923, the Arts Club of Chicago opened the first U.S. exhibition devoted solely to Pablo Picasso’s drawings. At a time when his fragmented faces and distorted bodies still scandalized much of the public, this small Midwestern club became an unlikely portal through which America met modern art’s revolution.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

current_fact.match_reason: Picasso’s early modern drawings embraced distortion and imperfection as truth, resonating with this episode’s celebration of rough edges and beauty beyond polish.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 1903, the first of several auctions of sheep-farming land in southern Patagonia began, reshaping who controlled the region’s vast grasslands. These sales accelerated a boom that transformed a windswept frontier into an industrial-scale landscape of wool and fences, often displacing Indigenous communities and early settlers in the process.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

queue.match_reason: The Patagonian land rush echoes many ‘Babylon’ stories of land, power, and displacement that roots reggae and dub reflect on, making this spacious, reflective set a fitting soundtrack.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 2010, Iceland’s Eyjafjallajökull volcano began a series of eruptions that would last for months and unexpectedly ground flights across Europe. Fine ash, invisible from the ground, drifted through air lanes and turned a remote glacier-covered volcano into a global traffic controller.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: Both the eruption and this episode live in the cold North Atlantic world of ice, fjords, and dramatic natural forces—one erupting skyward, the other plunging underwater among whales.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 1987, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved AZT (zidovudine), the first drug shown to slow HIV’s deadly progression. Approved in record time amid intense pressure from patients and activists, it marked a turning point where AIDS went from an unstoppable mystery to a disease that could, at last, be fought.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: The long, painful learning curve in understanding and treating HIV mirrors this episode’s focus on incremental progress, persistence, and science slowly turning confusion into hope.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 1815, Napoleon Bonaparte rode into Paris after escaping exile on Elba, greeted by cheering crowds and quickly assembling an army of over 300,000 men. This improbable comeback, known as the Hundred Days, briefly restored his rule and set the stage for one final, decisive clash at Waterloo.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

queue.match_reason: Napoleon’s dramatic late-career reinvention and brief return to the spotlight parallels this episode’s theme of transformation and evolving personas over time.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 1942, having just escaped the fall of the Philippines, U.S. General Douglas MacArthur stopped in the small Australian town of Terowie and declared, “I came out of Bataan and I shall return.” The improvised line, spoken from a dusty railway platform, became one of World War II’s most famous promises—and a personal mission he later fulfilled.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: MacArthur’s defiant, politicized vow to return to the Philippines aligns with the episode’s energy of resistance, conviction, and promises shouted against the backdrop of conflict.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 1922, the USS Langley was commissioned as the U.S. Navy’s first aircraft carrier, converted from a coal transport into a floating airfield. Its flat deck looked odd at the time, but it quietly pioneered the launch and landing techniques that would later define naval air power.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A pioneering ship that turned the sea into an airfield pairs nicely with an episode literally recorded at sea, both celebrating experimental journeys on the water’s surface.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 1921, the Upper Silesia plebiscite asked residents of an industrial borderland whether they wished to join Germany or Poland. The vote split along complex lines of language, class, and religion, and the contested results triggered uprisings and tense diplomacy before the region was finally partitioned.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

queue.match_reason: Both the plebiscite and this episode orbit Poland’s shifting borders and identity—one through a fraught 1921 vote, the other through a modern, personal journey across its landscapes.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 1616, after 13 years locked in the Tower of London, explorer and courtier Sir Walter Raleigh was unexpectedly released. Officially freed to lead an expedition in search of gold in South America, he stepped out of a fortress that had become a symbol of confinement and political danger.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: Raleigh’s exit from the Tower is a literal unboxing—a release from confinement into a new role—mirroring the episode’s theme of breaking out of imposed boxes and labels.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 1933, Nazi leader Heinrich Himmler ordered the establishment of Dachau, the first official concentration camp, near Munich. Initially presented as a place for ‘political prisoners’, it became a brutal prototype for a system of camps that would terrorize Europe for the next twelve years.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: The weight of Dachau’s creation calls for a gentle, reflective counterpoint; this slow, contemplative set offers space to sit with heavy history rather than drown it in noise.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 1848, amid the wave of revolutions sweeping Europe, King Ludwig I of Bavaria abdicated the throne in favor of his son. Scandals over his lavish spending on art and his relationship with a dancer mixed with demands for a constitution, turning a patron of the arts into a symbol of outdated monarchy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

queue.match_reason: A monarch toppled by a mix of simmering discontent and sudden revolutionary heat parallels this episode’s contrast between foggy restraint and volcanic southern fire.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 2015, Kurdish YPG fighters and allied Free Syrian Army units finally broke the months-long siege of Kobanê in northern Syria. Their defense of the small border city against ISIS became a global symbol of local resistance, gender equality in the ranks, and communities refusing to abandon their home.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: The desert-born, resistance-infused music of Tuareg artists resonates with Kobanê’s story of local fighters defending their land and identity in harsh, arid borderlands.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 2014, four gunmen disguised as diners opened fire inside Kabul’s Serena Hotel, killing at least nine people, including journalists and children. The attack pierced one of the Afghan capital’s most fortified ‘safe spaces’, underscoring how fragile the illusion of normalcy can be in a war-torn city.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A violent rupture inside a place meant for travelers and meetings contrasts starkly with this episode’s gentle travel-inspired reflection, inviting listeners to hold both the beauty and vulnerability of moving through the world.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 2000, former Black Panther H. Rap Brown—by then known as Jamil Abdullah Al-Amin—was captured in Georgia after a shootout that left one sheriff’s deputy dead and another critically wounded. Once a fiery civil-rights orator, his arrest linked the radicalism of the 1960s to ongoing debates about policing, race, and justice in the United States.

episode.title: Morning Drops #02 - Waves of the Maghreb
episode.description: Oriental vibes guided by Yasmin. Discovering curious facts about the Maghreb, together with its fascinating music! A wonderful trip with a selection of songs from North Africa.
songs: Nass El Ghiwane - El Madi Fate

queue.match_reason: Brown’s trajectory from Black Panther activism to later violence reflects global currents of resistance and exile; Maghreb music—especially songs like “Ya Rayah”—often tells similar stories of displacement and struggle from another shore.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 1990, Imelda Marcos, widow of former Philippine president Ferdinand Marcos, went on trial in New York on charges of bribery, racketeering, and embezzlement. The woman once known for her extravagant shoe collection now faced jurors over accusations that she looted millions from her country.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: Imelda’s opulent rise and public fall evoke the intense drama, pride, and lament that flamenco channels so well—music built for stories of glory, excess, and reckoning.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March, the March equinox often falls, marking the moment the Sun crosses the celestial equator and day and night are nearly equal worldwide. Ancient cultures tracked this balance point carefully, aligning temples and stone circles with the rising Sun as a reminder that the planet’s tilt is the metronome of seasons.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

queue.match_reason: The equinox is a cosmic hinge between seasons; this episode leans into the feeling of stretching one season—endless summer—through rhythm and light, mirroring that delicate balance point.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March, many parts of the world observe the vernal equinox as a time of cultural renewal, from Persian New Year (Nowruz) to various spring festivals. These celebrations link astronomy to everyday life, turning a precise celestial moment into rituals of cleaning, feasting, and starting over.

episode.title: Morning Drops #09 - Birthday Cake
episode.description: Today's breakfast comes with a sprinkle of stardust and a slice of birthday cake as we celebrate a pure and unique soul, Yasmin. On Sedna, we may not do years, but we sure know how to party!

queue.match_reason: Equinox festivals like Nowruz mark a planetary ‘birthday’ for spring; pairing it with a birthday-themed, celebratory episode highlights that shared mood of renewal and partying into a new cycle.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March, observers of the Moon sometimes witness a ‘supermoon’, when a full or new Moon occurs near its closest approach to Earth. The Moon appears subtly larger and brighter, inspiring photographers and poets even though the orbital mechanics behind it are rigorously mundane.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: The slightly larger, brighter ‘cosmic’ Moon overhead fits perfectly with an episode built on cosmic disco energy and spacey dance-floor momentum.

queue.fact_text: On 20 March 2011, NASA’s MESSENGER spacecraft slipped into orbit around Mercury, becoming the first probe ever to circle the innermost planet. For four years it mapped a world of shriveled cliffs, frozen polar shadow, and a surprisingly dynamic magnetic field only a few tens of millions of kilometers from the Sun.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: MESSENGER’s long, looping journey into a strange inner world parallels trip-hop’s cinematic drift into shadowy, boundary-blurring soundscapes—an orbiting exploration set to downtempo beats.

published.fact_text: On 20 March 1948, American television quietly crossed a cultural threshold when the Musicians Union lifted its ban and CBS and NBC broadcast full classical concerts conducted by Eugene Ormandy and Arturo Toscanini. For many viewers, it was the first time an orchestra entered their living room live, turning TV from novelty into a serious stage for high art.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: A landmark broadcast moment about bringing serious music into people’s homes pairs with Sedna’s own first broadcast: both are origin points where sound suddenly reaches a wider universe.

published.fact_text: On 20 March 1916, Albert Einstein submitted “The Foundation of the General Theory of Relativity” to Annalen der Physik, formally presenting the idea that gravity is not a force but the warping of spacetime. This paper reshaped how humans picture the cosmos, bending intuition as dramatically as massive stars bend light.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: An episode about reimagining ‘empty’ space as creative potential mirrors Einstein’s radical reframing of spacetime; both invite a shift in how we think about the void.

published.fact_text: On 20 March 2015, Earth lined up for a rare triple coincidence: a total solar eclipse, the March equinox, and a supermoon all fell on the same day. For a few hours, the Sun, Moon, and Earth danced in near-perfect alignment, casting an eerie twilight over parts of the Arctic and reminding skywatchers how celestial rhythms occasionally sync in spectacular ways.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: The eclipse–equinox–supermoon alignment has a slow cosmic build into a dramatic reveal, echoing this mix’s progression from mysterious ambience into a full, liberating groove.

published.fact_text: On 20 March 1964, European countries formalized their shared cosmic ambition by creating ESRO, the European Space Research Organisation. This collaborative agency became the test bed for Europe’s first scientific satellites and would later evolve into the European Space Agency, turning a patchwork of national programs into a unified presence in orbit.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: Both ESRO and this episode chart a collective journey into new territories—one through space research, the other through a shared voyage across musical galaxies.

published.fact_text: In the early hours of 20 March 1861, a violent earthquake struck Mendoza, Argentina, virtually erasing the city in less than a minute. The disaster not only claimed thousands of lives but also led to Mendoza being rebuilt with wider streets, plazas, and seismic-aware planning—an early example of a city redesigning itself around the lessons of geology.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden, devastating jolt from beneath the Earth matches with an episode themed around electric shocks and powerful surges of energy.

published.fact_text: On 20 March 1923, the Arts Club of Chicago opened the first U.S. exhibition devoted solely to Pablo Picasso’s drawings. At a time when his fragmented faces and distorted bodies still scandalized much of the public, this small Midwestern club became an unlikely portal through which America met modern art’s revolution.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

published.match_reason: Picasso’s early modern drawings embraced distortion and imperfection as truth, resonating with this episode’s celebration of rough edges and beauty beyond polish.
